questions.text: I tend to feel a surge of intense emotion before I have any cognitive understanding of what triggered it.
I find that emotional composure during hardship is what allows me to see the full picture rather than just the most painful part.
I save and invest with a long-term financial plan in mind.
I rarely notice when someone phrases a disagreement as a question to soften it.
I trust my gut instincts more than any amount of advance preparation.
My emotional system seems calibrated for maximum signal — nothing is filtered or dampened.
I notice that my emotional life is characterized by continuity rather than interruption.
I am energized by the act of making a consequential choice.
I find that aligning every aspect of my life with my personal mission statement keeps me centered.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering is the single most important consideration when evaluating the ethics of any decision, public or private.
I think of the present moment as the only real thing; the future is just a concept.
I do not usually register when someone deliberately avoids a topic in my presence.
I feel that my proudest moments are when someone tells me I made a difference in their life.
I feel most confident when I am contributing to a process others are co-directing.
I maintain separate spaces or systems for different areas of my life to avoid confusion.
I detect shifts in a group's mood almost instantly.
I detect when someone uses humor to say something true that they could not say seriously.
I believe a premature decision is more dangerous than a delayed one.
I tend to craft my daily schedule around what the people in my life need from me.
I prioritize tasks based on urgency and importance using a deliberate framework.
I am the type who builds the team around my vision rather than the other way around.
I tend to focus on the problem being discussed rather than on how people are feeling about it.
I feel that an open hand is always preferable to a closed fist, even when closing would protect my own interests.
I find that my tendency to approach suffering analytically has allowed me to be helpful in situations where others felt paralyzed.
I believe that my emotional processing happens largely beneath the surface, quietly and efficiently.
I feel that my strength lies in driving momentum.
I feel that the moral pull of another person's pain creates an imperative in me that no amount of reasoning can fully override or dismiss.
I find that my emotions have a physical weight — when I'm happy I feel lighter, when I'm sad I feel heavier.
I tend to measure a good day by how much progress I made on my own goals.
I notice I'm out of something and get more when I need to.
I detect when someone is diplomatically declining an invitation.
I believe that the moral concern I have for those in pain is not a weakness to be managed but a strength to be honored.
I feel that directing my resources toward my own goals is the smartest strategy.
I think that the most productive conversations about human tragedy are those conducted with analytical precision rather than emotional intensity.
I feel that my presence in a group helps others articulate their own priorities.
I experience change as something to be navigated calmly rather than something that triggers alarm.
